prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: a cheerful cartographer cat weaving a glowing fabric where one set of threads runs across a map of rivers and roads and the crossing threads tick forward like a row of tiny clocks, the two thread directions knitting into one shimmering cloth. Style friendly like XKCD or Kurzgesagt, one clear visual gag, generous negative space. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A cat weaves a fabric whose horizontal threads are a map and whose vertical threads are clocks, knitting space and time into one cloth.
caption: Spatio-temporal data is a single fabric woven from where things are and when they happen, and the whole book of this chapter is learning to read that cloth.

after_heading: 3. Representing Space: Grids, Graphs, and Coordinates Intermediate
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: on the left a tidy city of identical square tiles where neighbors are always the four touching squares, on the right a loose web of round stations connected by curving roads of wildly different lengths, a puzzled surveyor standing between them holding a ruler in one hand and a tangle of string in the other. Style friendly like XKCD or Kurzgesagt, one clear visual gag, generous negative space. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A surveyor stands between a neat square tile grid on one side and an irregular web of connected stations on the other, holding a ruler and a tangle of string.
caption: A grid assumes every neighbor sits one tidy step away, while a graph lets distance and connection vary, which is exactly why irregular space wants a graph and not a ruler.

after_heading: 2. The Architecture Pattern: Spatial Plus Temporal Intermediate
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: two friendly robot chefs working a relay, the first robot gossiping sideways with all its neighbors at a long round table to mix everyone's plates together, then sliding the blended dish to a second robot who lines up a tidy row of bowls stretching into the distance to taste how the flavor changes over the row. Style friendly like XKCD or Kurzgesagt, one clear visual gag, generous negative space. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: One robot blends plates with neighbors around a table, then hands the dish to a second robot tasting a long row of bowls that stretch into the distance.
caption: A spatio-temporal model splits the work in two: one operator mixes across neighbors in space, the other follows the signal along time, and stacking them is the whole recipe.

after_heading: 1. Video as Spatio-Temporal Data: Time Carries the Signal Beginner
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: a detective squinting at a single frozen photograph of a person mid-motion and shrugging in total confusion, while behind a curtain the same moment played as a smooth flip-book of frames clearly shows the person leaping, the detective oblivious to the obvious answer just out of view. Style friendly like XKCD or Kurzgesagt, one clear visual gag, generous negative space. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A detective is baffled by a single still photo of a blurred pose while a flip-book of the same motion behind a curtain plainly shows a leap.
caption: A single frame often cannot tell sitting down from standing up, because in video the meaning lives in the motion between frames rather than in any one of them.

after_heading: 3. Practical Challenges: Subjects, Labels, Imbalance, and the Wrist Advanced
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: a tiny earnest wristwatch sensor character riding on a person's wrist, sweating and overwhelmed as the same swooping arm gesture is produced by three wildly different activities at once, a chef whisking, a person waving hello, and someone brushing teeth, all blurring into one identical swirl the little sensor cannot tell apart. Style friendly like XKCD or Kurzgesagt, one clear visual gag, generous negative space. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A small overwhelmed wrist sensor watches three different activities, whisking, waving, and tooth brushing, all produce the same swirling arm motion.
caption: On the wrist, very different activities can trace nearly identical motion, which is why the placement of a single sensor quietly caps how well any classifier can do.

after_heading: 3. Scalability, Strong Baselines, and Foundation Models Advanced
prompt: A witty cartoon educational illustration with clean lines and a warm palette: a towering elaborate contraption of gears, pipes, and spinning antennae huffing and straining to finish a race, while a plain little wooden cart that simply repeats yesterday's load rolls calmly across the finish line first, the giant machine staring in stunned disbelief. Style friendly like XKCD or Kurzgesagt, one clear visual gag, generous negative space. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: An enormous overengineered machine of gears and antennae is beaten across a finish line by a plain little cart that just repeats yesterday's load.
caption: Again and again a humble baseline that mostly repeats the recent past beats the fancy graph network, which is why a strong baseline is the first thing any serious model must outrun.
